title: 01. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/course-expectations-142/


### Transcript

So let's add some course expectations. This course will give you the ATZ concept of website flipping. I'll walk you through the concept itself, the different stages that a website goes through to grow it and flip it. Two diligence valuations, quick tips on how to grow the site, how to position the site, and how to exit the site. So I'll give you pretty much ATZ. However, it's not a course where I cover the details of niche websites building, nor a course about SEO, CRL, etc. These are concepts I touch on, I talk about, and the importance of these topics. However, I'm not digging deep into any of these topics. There are a lot of excellent courses out there, YouTube videos, YouTube channels that cover these topics in very in depth. So I will just glance over them and cover them at a high level. Now, you'll be equipped with the knowledge to dig deeper. You'll know what the terminology is, what the keywords are, what the concepts are, such that you can now go and do more research. And I will provide a lot of real life examples, video walkthroughs, me doing live due diligence on deals, etc. Your mileage will vary, this industry is, you know, your buying businesses, websites, for a lot of things that will be different. You will be, you'll have challenges, some deals will go really easy, some won't, but you'll be equipped with the knowledge to take care of that. So that's the course expectations.

title: 02. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/my-qualifications-302/


### Transcript

So who am I? Why am I qualified to talk about website flipping? My name is Moshvik Sarger. I have been in the website flipping industry since 2008. In 2008 I started my own site from scratch just to generate some passive income while I was a sophomore in college. Now that's what started my journey. I started a website in the V.O.I.P. niche. So voice over internet protocol. I was essentially selling affiliate commissions for from Skype and other types of V.O.I.P services for calling internationally. It was very lucrative. I grew that site to about $1,000-$2,000 a month. That helped me go through college, essentially debt-free. I sold a site in early 2011, late 2010, for $25,000. That was my first experience in a website flip. Since then I have been buying and selling websites and I have done about $180,000, since 2008. I average about 10-15 flips per year. These are small flips, less than $10,000-$2,000-$6,000. I just replenish that money back into deals and continue growing sites and flipping them. Now I have been building a lot of tools sets for website investors. We will cover a few of these tools to help you in your website flipping journey. I am also the founder of the website flip.com, which is a dedicated newsletter covering case studies guides and deal flow analysis for website flippers. I have been featured in many places. Empire Flipers has articles on me. Flipah has articles, fat stacks, blog, niche pursuits. I have been doing all the podcasts and YouTube channels for website flipping and niche website building, including the Doug Show, niche pursuits podcast, and plenty of other YouTube channels. I am not just here to create a course for the sake of creating a course. I have the expertise. I do this on a daily basis, so I bring that knowledge and the new learnings and what I am learning in the industry back to you guys. Through this course, through my newsletter, and hoping to expand your industry as a whole, because digital assets are the future in my opinion. I am an excellent asset cluster in addition to typical real estate stocks and whatnot. Thanks for tuning in. Thanks for buying the course. I hope you find the value. If there are any questions, you can always reach out to me via email. Thanks so much.

title: 03. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/whats-covered/


### Transcript

Welcome to Module 1, Introduction to Website Flipping. In this module, you'll get many lessons on the basics of website flipping, the concept itself. Some common terminology in abbreviations. The website flipping industry encompasses a lot of different categories of expertise, and therefore that comes along with a lot of terms and abbreviations that are just hard to keep up with. So, life creator lesson just to cover those. How to set your investment criteria. Website flipping is a business. You have investments and therefore you have to have a set criteria that you follow. Then to really just show you guys the different types of flips I have done, I put data to spreadsheet with data from 39 website flips from 2018 to about early 2021. It gives you all the raw data so you can just look at the different price points, the what multiples, how long it took to sell, etc. Then a common question that a lot of people ask is, why should I buy a website when I can just build one from scratch, or why should I build one when I can just buy one from a marketplace. So the build prefers by situation here is very common in this industry as well. And then what are the required skillsets to be a successful website flipper? I list out a lot of the skills that I have collected over the years, seen people who are successful in website flipping, what kind of skills they have, and while they are not really required per se, you can always hire experts. It's good to have an understanding of these skillsets at the very least. Then website flipping is an investment, and therefore financial modeling is something everyone should do to understand the potential ROI that's possible. And so I have open sourcing provided my financial models spreadsheet, which is a portfolio model. So models for sites acquired by an individual or a investors with an individual, and it has costs breakdown for employees, and everything else you can think about, so that you can model all the scenarios on a three-year timeline. It's very detailed, potentially overkill for some situations, but you can customize it to whatever situation that falls in your website flipping a journey. So these lessons will just get you started, and put you on the right path to really understanding how to flip a website.

title: 04. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/website-flipping-concept-226/


### Transcript

In this lesson, I'm going to cover the website flipping concept. Now, I like to break it down into four major steps. First, you either buy or create a website. Ideally, in this kind of a course, you're actually buying the website from a different brokerage or marketplace, but creating a website also works. You then grow the traffic to the website. You add monetization that can be affiliate marketing, display ads, lead generation, digital products, consulting, newsletters, anything really. Then you prepare the site for sale and you list it and you actually exit the website. Very high level, four steps, four step process here. Now, with the case study, we acquired a site in the dating niche in April 2019. It was from Flip-A, earning about $800 to $1000 a month. We bought it at $37,000 price point. It was not an auction, which usually Flip-A is a marketplace for auctions. It was direct message through their platform for a buy-it-now offer. We prefer not going down the auction route because it causes a lot of bidding wars and last minute bidding and stress. So we'd like to just give an offer if we're very confident on the website. Now, I bought the site in April 2019, kept it until February 2020. So less than a year, and we grew the site, my team and I, from about $1000 a month to $6,000 a month average. We sold the site in February 2020, $494,000. So that's an example of a perfect Flip. Less than a year, we bought a distressed asset. We grew the traffic to the website, but more importantly, we monetized the website better than the previous owners. We held on to the site until the average earnings was good enough for us so that we could get a good exit, which was at $6,000 average. And then we found a buyer who was willing to buy that at $194,000 full cash. So that's a perfect case study for a showcasing what the website's flipping concept is. It encompasses a lot of skill sets, which we'll cover in the future slides, but in general, four major steps.

---

title: 05. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/terminology-abbreviations-225/


### Transcript

terminology and abbreviations. Now this is critical. Website flipping is multi-dimensional. You have M&A, mergers and acquisitions, SEO, switch engine optimization, conversion rate optimization, a CRO, growth, team management, pretty much all aspects are involved in the process of website flipping. So there's a ton of terminology, a ton of abbreviations for each of these categories. Now the most critical ones that really you need to know in terms of buying and selling a site are listed here. For example, do diligence now. You know that's essentially the research that you do as a buyer to confirm all the information from the sellers perspective when they list a site for sale. That could be do diligence on the backlink profile, on the revenue, a P&L spreadsheet, the screen shots, the site history, all of that's part of do diligence. Now you'll see some terms put out there like L3M, L6M, L12M, TTM. Now these are all short form for essentially tied to the revenue or the profit of the business. So L6M is usually used on when we do valuations and that's essentially last six months, last six months average earnings, L6M. TTM is trailing 12 months essentially all kind of the same concepts here. The next thing is multiple. You'll see you know people talk about hey I bought it at 35X multiple or I bought it at 3X multiple. Now these are all different terms for the multiplication of earnings that you equates to the sale price. So the valuation process is your earnings times the multiple equals your sales price and so you know people say oh I bought it at 35X which is monthly 35X times monthly earnings or annual earnings would be like two and a half X, three X, three and a half X. So there's just different ways to say it and different industry terminologies but pretty much multiple means you know what you paid for the business and I'm going to put a huge list of all the terminology abbreviations below this video written formats so you can just go over it and glance over it when you need it.

title: 06. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/setting-your-investment-criteria-1010/


### Transcript

In this lesson, I'm going to cover how to set your investment criteria. In typical investments like real estate stocks, you're always putting together a criteria. For example, you're not going to buy multi-family homes. You're only going to buy single-family homes. You need a specific cash on cash return to make sense. You're only going to buy a few deals a year in this kind of location. That's the investment criteria. The same applies to content-based websites. You need to have a detailed investment criteria such that when you go searching for deals, you know which ones are ever interested and which ones aren't. This saves you time and ensures that you have a proper due diligence process and you're not going away from what goals you have set. Now, for content websites, I have divvied up into eight key criteria. First is your type of website. Are you going to buy a content site, a lead-gen site, a services business, etc. Is it going to be on WordPress? Is it going to be on Shopify? Now, these are all important things to consider. Next is the specific niches you want to enter. It's important to have some interest in it, not always necessary, but you need to understand that I'm going to enter a bc niche. I'm not going to enter xyz. Traffic sources. What's your expertise? Are you going to do organic traffic, social traffic, email traffic? Just make sure you understand that a specific traffic source has different monetization structures in place as well. Traffic minimums, right? Do you need traffic to be more than 50,000 page views per month or are you okay to have a site with 10,000 page views per month? This is all different levels of traffic that have different challenges associated with it. Revenue sources. The existing revenue source that is going to be affiliate marketing, display ads, lead generation, digital products. You have to understand what is your core competency and target deal flow with that kind of monetization structures. Monthly revenue. Now, we have limited time in our day. You may have a full time job and you're just looking for site projects to acquire some sites. Therefore, you need to have a certain minimum monthly revenue requirement such that it motivates you to do your job, to grow the site. Usually it could be a thousand dollars a month. That is enough for you to have enough cash flow such that you pay yourself and you have enough money to be invested to the site. Or it could be a hundred dollars a month. It just depends. Website age. Are you going to buy a website that's less than a year old, 10, 20 years old? This is all criteria that you need to put into consideration as well. Now backlink types. This is something more in-depth, more advanced. However, are you okay with buying sites that have private blog network links or that have guest post links? Are you okay with looking for only links that are journalistic, press release and those kind of high quality links? This is also a criteria to consider. However, for some beginners, this may be a bit advanced and it can be ignored for the time being. So that's the eight different criteria for you to think about. Now I'm going to share my investment criteria and then with that you can put together your investment criteria that makes sense for you. So my investment criteria. Now in general, the more relaxed your criteria is, the more deals you will review. The more dealful you'll have access to. For example, if you're only looking for sites that are 20 years old, based on my experience, you might get only one or two sites a year to even look at. If you're looking for sites that are earning $20,000 a month, our clean backlink profile has a million page views. That's very strict criteria. You will find deals but it will just be very limited. So I use a very relaxed criteria that leads to me to have more at a deal flow, which means I'm spending a lot of time going through that deal flow, but it also gives me the opportunity to learn, to hone in my skills on due diligence and also find maybe a needle in the haste that I didn't know about. So here's my criteria. My type of website. I'm only looking for content blogs, content sites built on WordPress. For WordPress is my go-to. I'm an expert in it. I've been using it for more than a decade. I know the ins and outs of it. I know every plugin, the... I have freelancers and virtual assistants that understand WordPress. So it's very easy for me to build on that ecosystem. Now I'm not niche specific. I am niche agnostic. I have owned my skillset that I know I can monetize any website in any niche. I just will stay away from spamming niches, adult niches, gambling niches, because those just doesn't fit my persona. Traffic sources. Now I'm only targeting sites with organic traffic. And I need the site owners to have Google Analytics access so that I can get access into their... As a guest and verify all analytics data. That's my criteria. I know many buyers that look for social media sites like Pinterest sites and then add on organic traffic because that can be done as well. But their core competency is Pinterest. So it just depends on what you are comfortable with. Now traffic minimums. I'm looking for sites that at least get 1000 pay views per month. That's very, very low and that can be put into the category of starter sites. And I don't have a problem with that because this again is a relaxed criteria. I may only buy sites that have 50,000 pages, but I want to see all the deals. Now revenue sources. I'm an expert in affiliate marketing, display ads and digital products. So I'm looking for something in the affiliate sites and some digital products that I can tag on, display ads as an additional revenue source or vice versa. I'm looking for something with digital products that I can add affiliate marketing to and display ads. So it's just these kind of different variations that I want to look out for. Now for the site, I'm looking for at least $100 a month over the last three months. Again, relaxed criteria. I have to see a lot of easy wins on the site at $100 a month such that I can take that to maybe $500 a month or $1000 a month within next month. And that makes me buy the business. And I'm also looking at a site that has a growing or steady trend over earnings. I don't want to see a peak in one month and then $0 a next month. That to me is not a very steady site. And those kind of sites actually cause more issues to motivation. When you see a site that has a huge peak and the next month it's not earning anything, that actually is an indication that it's an affiliate and a affiliate site that had a big sale and you've got the affiliate permission the next month you're not earning anything, which for beginners it can be a demotivating factor is that you're not consistently earning revenue so you feel like your website is dying and you're not making any money. Now age, I'm looking for sites that are one year old or older. Anything less than a year old is what I've put into as a starter site and those are sites that are coming out of the Google Sandbox. They're coming out. It's starting to get their rankings and I don't want to deal with those. I want to deal with a site that has a time to marinate essentially. Now for backlink types, I'm looking for sites that don't have any private bulk network links or any family links like Chinese links, Russian links, adult casino gambling type links. And the reason I don't buy private bulk network sites, I know a lot of buyers that actually target PBN sites because they have direct expertise in managing PBN type links. Maybe they switch out the PBNs for more high quality links so that site is much safer. I personally don't deal with it because in the past I have been burned. I bought a PBN site that got penalized and so I just decided that's not for me and so I still do it. I still clear of that. So that's my criteria. Your criteria will be different. It's really on a personal preference. I know people who want to invest $100,000 to $200,000 in a website and their criteria is more strict. The site has to probably earn $2,000 to $3,000 a month because that's the minimum revenue requirement they need to get involved. I know people who are looking for strategic acquisitions. Maybe they have an agency already that offers SEO services or some kind of consulting services in the industry and they're looking for a content site to help grow that business. For example, a lawyer made a law firm and they're looking for a legal site content site to acquire so that they can share content, target an audience that might buy their services as a law firm. Those are strategic acquisitions and those are more powerful and more profitable. So it really depends on your situation. So take some time to understand the investment criteria. Put it together. Write it down. Save it. And then adjust over time as you grow in the website's living journey.

title: 08. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/building-vs-buying-niche-sites-327/


### Transcript (Part 1)

The age-old bi-versus build issue. Do you buy a site, a business, or do you build it from scratch? This is evident in content websites as well. Somebody had to build a site that you were going to buy. So what are the pros and cons to building websites to flip? I've been in this industry for 12 years now, so I have built sites. I have bought and then grown and sold sites. I have done everything. So I will share my honest opinion and then you can make a decision for yourself. So what are the pros and cons of building websites to flip? Now, when you build something, you get an understanding from start to finish on how that site is working, the content is working, what kind of links are working, how long it takes for a content to rank. So there's an intricate understanding that you build over time. Now, that's one of the major pros is you have exact control of the on-page SEO, the site structure, the keywords you're going to target, the topics you're going to enter, etc. That control is very valuable for sure. You have a strict control on costs when you build. You can invest $1,000 here next month, zero, and kind of control that process. When you build from scratch, you can also buy sites, ready-made starter sites from DUNFORU services like agencies. But when you build from scratch, it's much cheaper. Obviously it takes some time, but it's much cheaper than buying a DUNFORU website from an agency or even buying a revenue-generating content site as well. So the barrier to entry is very low. These are the major pros. No, there's some major cons as well. If you are getting just started, it does require you to understand how to set up a proper niche site, how to structure the content, how to structure the theme, which plugins to use so that can be overwhelming for a lot, and they may require training and education and some courses to kind of understand that process. And then it requires you to do it yourself, everything from scratch, right? Either you hire people, you hire agencies, you hire writers to essentially help you build this website from scratch. Now, caveat here. If you're just getting started in a website flipping, I highly, highly, highly recommend building a website from scratch, seeing how it works, and then hopefully selling that website as well and seeing that process before you get active in buying and selling. Now, it's all time-dependent and how much capital you have to invest, but it's recommended at least give it a shot if you're on your own first. Now, who is it right for building websites to flip? It's somebody who has the know-how, the team, and the time to essentially outsource part of the website or build it from scratch themselves and who can actually go through that process. Not everybody has that kind of flexibility, especially time, so it's not always the best for every individual, so that's why buying a website may be a better fit.


### Transcript (Part 2)

In the previous video, I covered building websites to then flip. Now, obviously, the other side of that is buying websites. Now, what are the pros and cons to this? I am a strong proponent of buying websites. So, I'll try to share my unbiased opinions here. So, pros, you know, you get to acquire a site that's been aged on someone else's server and time. Right? You may buy a three or four-year-old site. And if you were the bill that you would have to wait three or four years, you can kind of get access to a lot of deals that are aged, that also potentially have quality math links and a ton of content. So, definitely it's a time-saver in that sense. The site may already be earning revenue. The revenue is actually the biggest motivator to get started in anything. You know, if the site's making $500 a month, day one, after you buy it, you're motivated to work on it, to take that to the next level. And it's also generating enough cash flow to cover your costs, to grow it, and also potentially a small stipend for yourself, so you can enjoy the proceeds. Now, one very important factor here is that when you build a site from scratch, you have to hire your team, specifically content writers, and you're paying them retail rates. If you're finding people on Upwork, maybe you're using an agency, you can expect to pay anywhere for an agency. You can expect to pay anywhere for an English-speaking quality writer, $5 up to $0.15 per work, and those are retail rates. Now, when you buy a content site from a brokerage or direct from the end user, you're actually buying the content at a discount. So, valuation metrics, calculations, do not factor in how much actual content is on the website. You get a website that has 100, 200,000 words, and you're getting that a significant discount, because website valuations are done on revenue only. So, there's a disconnect there, and that's actually a benefit for website flippers, because we can buy this bulk content at very, very low costs. So, essentially wholesale costs. Now, there's costs, obviously, right? You don't understand what the website creator, the one you're buying, did to the site. What kind of links they build? What kind of content they may have added in the past or now, and they removed what kind of topics they're covering? In addition, something even more technical is what kind of plugins they're using, what kind of theme they're using, what kind of customizations they did that you now have to learn. So, there's a steep learning curve. And if you're not technically savvy, you need to find some help to help you understand the site structure and kind of walk you through that process. So, the next con is obviously when you're building from scratch, you have full control. But when you're buying, you need to do detailed due diligence, right? To actually answer all these questions. What kind of content they add to the site? How is it monetized? Is it consistent monetization over time? All of those things are more questions that you need to go through. But it's definitely can be seen as a con and it requires a lot more effort. So, again, the ideal individual is somebody like me, right? Who has done this so many times, had built sites from scratch. But somebody who also has access to capital to deploy. When I say capital, it doesn't need to be a lot of money. You can buy a website for less than $10,000 up to a lot higher than that millions, right? So, the sweet spot for most website flippers is less than $100,000. You can buy something, grow it, and then sell it again for much higher profit. And that's what most website flippers are doing. Anything higher than that, and you're getting into more other people's using other people's money, private equity firms, and whatnot. And the second thing is you've got to understand website operations, right? When you build from scratch, you kind of understand who needs to be hired, what kind of tasks they need to do. So, when you're buying something, if you don't have that understanding at a basic minimum, you need to learn quickly, which a lot of people do. It's not rocket science, it can definitely be done. But you have to understand what goes into building a website, growing the website, what kind of people are needed. And so, that's essentially called a website operations. So, anyways, to summarize, pros and cons of buying versus building will always be there. The good strategy here is to combine the two. You can always buy a few sites, use that as for cash flow, grow those, and use the cash extra profits from that to build sites where you can go through that process as well. And so, it's always an evolution here of how many you build, how many you buy. But buying definitely has its major pros, and I'm a proponent of it. Because you get the motivation day one. You can see the easy wins quickly and immediately apply growth strategies to it to then take the revenue higher and have a very profitable asset that you can then sell. That can't really be done when you're building. It takes much longer to do. So, that's the pros and cons. And the decision here is really up to you. But I really recommend that you consider both strategies in your criteria.

title: 09. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/the-required-skillsets-528/


### Transcript

Website flipping in and of itself has a good amount of required skill sets. You can outsource a lot of these skill sets but it's good for you as the Website flipper to have an understanding at a high level of all the skill sets so that you can hire the right person. Now, I like to coin Website flippers as Jack of all trades, Master of None. We have an understanding in all these categories but we are not supposed to be an expert in every single one of these. It helps to be an expert but it's not a requirement. When I got started in Website flipping in 2008, I was not an expert in any of these. I learned everything on the while doing flips and building sites so definitely it can be learned and there's much more education available today than there was in 2008 so it's a much better situation for beginners to enter the market. Now, the different categories I have here, such an optimization. If you're buying a site with organic traffic, you have to understand how to do keyword research on Pages. CLL, Pages. CLL, outreach, for partnerships and backlinks. These are all part of the SEO category essentially. Any amount of tization, you obviously have to generate revenue, so you have to find opportunities, ability to test those opportunities, set up unique partnerships and then analyze data to understand which monetization stream is working, which one is in, which one you should improve upon and which one you should stop doing. Then a subset of that is conversion rate optimization. Where should you place elements on your site? For example, call tax and buttons, tables, links to maximize the revenue potential from a single visitor coming to your site which is called conversion rate optimization. Now, all of these search engine optimization monetization and conversion rate optimization requires data analytics skill set. Now, that's, you know, it's not very advanced data analytics, you know, it's spreadsheets, running numbers, calculations, so it's very simple, but you have to understand that you have to make decisions based on data. Then you have on the, you have website designed, technology stack. That's very critical, right? You're building a website on server, you're hosting it on the server, then you're using content management system like WordPress to host your content. Then you have design on top of that, which are themes, plugins and services you use, right? So it's very critical to have an understanding of that ecosystem, which theme works best, which plugins work best and how to improve your page speed. These are all important things that as a website flip, where you have to have a basic level of technology understanding. You can definitely easily outsource these things overseas. It's there's plenty of top talent that understands this and can help you along the way. Now, M&A, when you're buying selling websites, you're buying selling businesses, you are doing mergers and acquisitions. You have to understand how to read a P&L, while a P&L for a content site is fairly simple compared to a brick and mortar retail business, but still you have to understand how to put that together, how to analyze that, how to look at trends. You have to understand due diligence. You have to understand what's the levers that a website can improve upon, what are the things that can manipulate it, like bad backlink profiles, etc. You have to be able to find deals. You have to know where to look for deals. You have to have a process for analyzing deals, so you don't waste too much time. Then you have to understand team management. All these things can be outsourced. All these categories can be outsourced. I highly recommend that you as a website flipper go through the process at least a few times so you understand what can be outsourced and what you should be doing. You have to be able to find talent on sites like Upworkfreelancer.com, Facebook groups, agencies. You have to onboard those people so that they understand that they're part of a team. They're building something with you. You have to have SOPs, operating procedures to teach that individual how to do specific tasks so that you can focus on the next thing that will move the needle for your business. In short, website flippers are Jack of all trades, Master of None. When I started in 2008, I learned everything from scratch. I didn't have an expertise in any of these specific industries, categories. It just over time I learned everything and had a good understanding now that I can be dangerous. I can't claim myself as an expert in any one of these things. All along the way I've learned how to find experts that when I need them through Upwork or different sites to hire these freelancers on a contractual basis or maybe on an hourly or a fixed rate structure. To do specific tasks for me, so the ability to find that talent is very critical as well. So the required skill sets are vast. However, they can all be learned over time as you progress in your website flipping journey.

title: 11. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/whats-covered-1/


### Transcript

Welcome to Module 2 where I will discuss the website flipping lifecycle. Now this lifecycle is a framework that I have developed over the years. Essentially it's broken down into five stages. The first stage is buying a site, second is stabilizing the site, third is growing the site, fourth is optimizing for the exit and fifth is the execution of the exit. Now this framework will allow you to take a choir site and go all the way to exit with step by step processes. In this module I will briefly cover each of the stages and what it entails and some details around it. There will be subsequent modules breaking down each of these stages into further details.

title: 12. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/stage-1-buying-a-site-306/


### Transcript

Within the website flip lifecycle you have stage one which is behind a site. The purpose is to find opportunities for your next acquisition, essentially a deal flow. Now there's many ways to buy a site, many strategic ways and many ways through brokers and marketplaces. The best deals are going to come from off-market deals that you find on your own, either outreaching to blogs that you find on Google search results that match your criteria or by getting connected with an individual you've had an acquaintance with over time who's willing to sell their site. Those are going to be the best deals, you'll have the most room for negotiation, there won't be a broker in the middle taking 5 to 20% of fees for just connecting you. Then you have brokers, plenty of brokers like on the right hand side here on the slide deck event, higher flippers which is one of the biggest brokerages. You have motion invest, you have quiet light investors club, FE International which are all traditional brokerages and then you have marketplaces like Flippa which are like eBay of the website investing world where anyone can list a business for sale and it's an auction based format. You also have the website flip, we are a brokerage as well. We usually have a newsletter that sends out deals every week and within those deals we sometimes feature our own deals that we're brokering. Now the benefits of using a broker is they've mostly vet the deal for you from at least the first instance, they don't list everything, it's still up to you to do the dealages but at least they do some sort of vetting. With Flippa you can, there's no vetting involved like in depth, anything can be listed. However that also provides opportunity where you can find the needle in the haste at deal because most people know about Flippa that are willing to sell a business and they're going to list there just to try things out and you might be able to find good deals from there and we found many of our good deals from Flippa. Now what's a good deal when you're buying a site under monetized or under optimized? These are two things, under monetized means there might be revenue streams that are missing that you can tack on, under optimized means they didn't do any proper SEO or maybe they didn't optimize for social, etc. Something you see that the buyer, the seller does not see. A low competition niche you might find a lot of keywords that the seller missed and under value deal, right? You were able to negotiate down from the average multiple, the average multiple let's say is 35 or 40X monthly, you're able to get it at 30X that's that you have an instant equity in the deal. Now that's stage one, in the following size I'm going to cover the other stages within the website Flippa cycle and then the next few modules you can dig deep into each of these stages.

title: 13. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/stage-2-stabilizing-the-site-636/


### Transcript

So this is stage two, which is stabilizing the site site. In stage one, you actually bought the site. You went through finding the deal, the right deal, and actually acquired it. And now you are the owner. So in stage two, you actually start working on the site. But the first step is really just understanding the site and stabilizing the site. Here are eight tasks that I do. Every single time I buy a site, and it lasts, the stage lasts about two to four weeks. Depending on your ability to work on the site. But usually in a month, I like to stabilize the site. Now there are many tasks that go to stabilizing the site. Some of them take a lot of time. Some of them are very quick. Let's walk through them. So switching our monetization to your accounts. This is a no-brainer. You're buying the site for its revenue. And so you need to switch out affiliate tags. You need to switch out display add advertisements to your account. Maybe even if there's lead generation or digital products, you need to switch out the payment platforms, etc. You should move the site to a very good web host. I love cloudways. I'm a big fan. There's also WPX hosting, KINSTA, all these very high quality servers that you can use. Now the next is to improve the foundation of the website. This is critical. The foundation is your key to growth. And if you don't have a proper theme, plugins, or the website is bloated with plugins, it's just going to slow your website down. So either you can do this yourself if you have the skillsets to understand which plugins are necessary, which are not, or hire an expert to optimize your website for page speed. Then ensure proper analytics and tracking. The data is key. Data is the one thing that we have access to that we can make decisions out of on our website flip. So make sure you have Google Analytics installed. If you want to understand where people are actually scrolling and clicking, you can install a heat map software like hot jar to know what people are doing on your site. The next thing is if the theme is outdated, if the design is outdated, which can be the case on websites that are many, many years old, or websites that are created by individuals that don't have a Mac for design, you can do a site design, the redesign. Now this is optional again. I usually like to do that no matter what. I have my own technology stack set of themes and plugins that I use, and I just put that on the website as soon as I buy it, because it allows me to save time working on the site on a daily basis. Now those were five tasks that are critical. Those five tasks are something I do on every single site, no matter what, just to improve the basic foundation. The remaining three, step six through eight, are from SEO task or revenue tasks that you can do. You can delay them slightly, however I recommend doing it within the one month. Performance site content audit. When you buy a site, you are buying legacy content. The previous owner added hundreds of articles potentially that maybe they're not a SEO expert, and they were just writing for the sake of writing. And so it's necessary to do a content prune, content audit to understand which articles you should delete, which articles you should redirect, which articles you should just keep as is. Then it's time to also find dedicated writers and our content agencies. Your content site, the lifeblood of it is content, right? And publishing content on a frequent basis. So you need to either write it yourself, but if you're really building a portfolio, you need to be able to find dedicated writers or content agencies that you trust and know the quality of. So you can use tools like Upwork, which is where I find my dedicated writers, or you can use, you know, wrote up referrals for content agencies that you can find on Facebook groups that people have vetted. There's a ton of content agencies out there. Some are very good. Some are not so good. So make sure to do your two diligence on the content agencies as well. Lastly, it's to implement the major easy wins. Now, there's a lot of easy wins you can do on a site, but there are a few that you can do to really move the needle, right? And this is site dependent examples. If you buy an affiliate site that's monetized by Amazon Associates, which is very common to find for sale, you can immediately tack on display advertisements like izoic, media line, ad drive, or good-of-google adsense. But this will just immediately give you an boost in revenue that the previous owner may have missed. Vice versa, if your site is monetized with display ads, but it has potential for adding affiliate links via Amazon Associates, share a sell, commission junction, and I have on link. Now, these are all networks with a lot of different brands under it. You can add these affiliate links to your display ad content and get a boost in revenue as well. Other things are major easy wins. I like to use the 80-20 principle in everything I do. 80% of your traffic is coming to 20% of your articles, top articles. So focus on those. Look through the top 10, 15 articles, and improve those drastically. It could be a design change. It could be adding to display ads and affiliate links. It could be adding comparison tables if it's a product based affiliate site. Or it could be a simple restructuring of the content so it's easier to read and easier to understand, and it's less bloated. These things can just move the needle quickly so that you get a boost in revenue which motivates you to continue working on the site, and also gives you cash flow that you can reinvest into the site. So in short, take a few weeks, stabilize the site, improve the foundation, get those easy wins on the books, and that's just going to set you up for the next stage of the website Flip Life Cycle.

title: 14. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/stage-3-growth-906/


### Transcript

In the previous stage, we talked about stabilizing the site, essentially to prepare it for the growth stage, which is stage 3. Now the purpose of this is to feel the fire. Now the reason I say that is because when you buy a content site, it already has a lot of traction. It's been aged in terms of Google's eyes. It's out of the sandbox, it's earning revenue, it has content. It's a living, breathing entity and you are going to feel that fire, continue that growth. So there's a handful of tasks, six of them that I highly recommend everybody do in the growth stage and focus only on these and ideally not the other things which I'll cover in tasks to avoid. So let's get started, right? So optimizing existing content for better on page SEO. Previous owners, previous creators, they may not be an SEO expert, they may be a hobby blogger or maybe they do have some SEO expertise, but they just they're just getting started. If SEO is your expertise, this is just even better because you should go through the top 20 pages or so and improve the on page, add more keywords to headings, add more heading sections that you can target for long tail keywords, add a frequently asked question section, improve the formatting of the page, improve essentially the time on page to give a better experience to the end user. All of this is critical and will set you up for that exponential growth on the existing content. You can always add adding more content is always ideal and that's what a lot of people do, but taking advantage of content you already have on your site, that's a low hanging fruit right there. The second one, adding new content. Obviously that's I mean the goal here to put more content on the site, that's quality and targets low competition keywords such that you start bringing in more traffic. Now how do you find these keywords? There are plenty of tutorials online on how to do keyword research, however, those two tools I use is ahrefs and keyword chef.com. These allow me to bundle those two together to find a bunch of keywords that I can target. I usually do a lot of keyword research early on in the website flipping my cycle, so after I buy the site before the growth stage I'm doing keyword research to make a huge list so that I will have to repetitively do that. Then I can just target one at a time as I need them. Now three, now that you have already improved the on pages here of your existing content, then you are adding new content. You have to go back and link all your old content together, all your new content together and essentially improve the internal linking. I use a tool called Link Whisper to help me with this process. It expedites the ability to find those internal linking opportunities and I also use common sense to link the content together. Internal links in my opinion are very powerful if not as powerful as external links essentially back links pointing to your site. Now another thing is we talked about step one to improve the on page of existing content, but there is also improving the on page of your whole site overall. This could be improving the navigation menu so it's easier for users to find content, improving the categories. One thing I see a lot of lot of sizes, sellers typically like to have a lot of irrelevant categories where things could have been bundled but they chose to separate it out. I like to keep it very tight knit, a few categories and that's very topically relevant to the main site. So I go to a category audit to remove categories to add new categories that are relevant and to organize the content in a better fashion. Now five, adding new revenue sources. Now this is the ideal goal. What you're looking for is obviously in stage two of the stabilization we added some easy made revenue opportunities like adding display ads and adding affiliate links. But in this situation you're trying to find new sources on top of that. Can you add a direct affiliate partnership maybe through Share or Cell you find a brand that you're already ranking for some of their keywords. They have a good product. You can now put their product on the landing page itself. So what I do in this situation, I take the top ten articles on my site and see what opportunities are there. If it's a mix of informational articles, what is the intent of those articles? Are they trying, is a user trying to find a solution and is there a product or a service or something that can solve that and I can become an affiliate and essentially partner with them and get my commissions. So just look at the top ten, look for the opportunities, add in relevant products and then that's a new revenue stream that you just introduced. Not all of these revenue streams will be successful so you always have to collect the data, understand it, remove the sources that don't work at new ones and just continue this process. Now six is optimizing for conversions, right? Again, you've improved the content but you didn't improve the CRR which is conversion and optimization. Our goal is to take more advantage of the visitors that are coming to our site in order to generate more revenue. But people who are coming to our site already click through from Google, they are very likely to potentially take the next step which is by. So our goal is to make it easier for them to find the information they need such that they click through and buy whatever we recommend or our own products. So the way to do that is just go through each of the top pages that get traffic and improve the CRR that could be adding call to action buttons in the right locations, that could be adding a comparison table that's very compact straight into the point that could be adding links within the content itself so it's easier for them to click through. Something like that will help the user find the information they need and then take the next step. Now that's six steps right there. That's very critical and this can be done in out of order or in order. It doesn't really matter, it's just I do this simultaneously. Now the growth phase is these major steps done on a two to six month period and I'm continuing to add more content, optimizing that content, optimizing existing content and finding new ways to generate revenue. Pretty much that sums it up and there are things that I do avoid and I think that's very important to touch on during this growth phase. Remember you bought a living breathing site that has been live. Most likely it already has some backlinks, it already has traffic obviously because it's earning revenue. So the goal with this growth phase is to maximize that fuel the fire essentially. So I like to avoid building backlinks in this growth phase which is two to six month time period. backlinks are great to have, I'm a strong proponent of them. However when you add them on to during the growth phase it takes time for backlinks to kick in essentially for Google to understand the value and to give you new traffic. So in the growth phase I like to focus on what is available on the site today. What do I have to work with and improve that. Same with that said is I don't like to work on new traffic sources because a new traffic source is a new skill set that I may need to learn or hire somebody for and if your sites are already getting organic traffic which is great. I just like to feel that fire to bring more organic traffic and focus on new traffic sources later because you might spend a lot of time up front on new traffic sources but they may not be the benefits. So that's not to say you can't do those two. If you have a big team and you have a big budget you can hire people to help you with all these things and you can do everything simultaneously. How I'm talking about a solo operator with maybe a few virtual assistance freelancers when needed and they're building up their first site or a portfolio of a few sites and you need to really focus on that stage because you have other things going on. You may have a full time job, you may have a family so you have to focus and these are the six things that I highly recommend to focus between month two to month six essentially of the growth phase.

title: 15. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/stage-4-exit-optimization-539/


### Transcript

Stage 4, exit optimization. I love this stage because it's time to think about how and when you're going to sell the site. In stage 1, we talked about stabilizing the site, getting it ready. Stage 2 was the massive growth phase when we're feeling the fire, investing more into the website such that it grows. Now in stage 4, we are coming out of the growth phase where we may be at the tail end of the growth phase and we're getting ready to understand what's the next step here. So that's the exit optimization. So sites are sold on a L6M. We talked about this before is the last six months average earnings. So you need to give time to showcase growth. If you sell to early within maybe two months after you buy the site, you won't have enough of a L6M average earnings to make sense. The growth may not have been that high and the average earnings would be low. So it's really critical to optimize for this exit. There's two types of exits that I really talk about. There's the exit strategy 1 which is midway exit. Essentially, you are on a growth curve which is the best case situation. You want the site and you're growing month over month over month and you can exit maybe at the tail end of that growth curve. Maybe after three or four months of growing, you see that this is a good enough last six month average earnings because you're going to take a last six month average from here and your average is going to be somewhere in the middle and you think that's good enough for you in a enough of a return on investment such that you do a midway exit. So you're selling on the growth curve. There's a lot of pros of selling on a midway exit. The buyer on the other end who you're going to sell the site to is excited because they see the site is growing. They're paying a low average but the consequent months after they buy the site is going to keep growing hopefully or get a new peak every single month. So they're happy they're buying a growing site. The growing site means in general a healthy site in a way and so the buyer is always happy. Now there's the con here that you are potentially selling it for less than its worth because the last six month average is lower than if you held on to it and let the average work itself up. However, it's always a risk management right. If you have another product that you want to invest in, if you want to sell this because you are not interested in this site and you want to prepare funds for a next acquisition, it's always good to plan early and sell early. Now the next one is stabilized exit. This is an ideal for you and which is weighed until the last six months has stabilized to a value that makes sense for you and I like to say it is if a site has stabilized and you can't see ways to grow it, it's time to sell it. If your expertise are capped, there's nothing you can do to take that site forward. You're potentially going to lose interest over time and you have to upkeep the site. So if you're buying other sites while this one is sitting in on your servers and not getting time it needs over time, that site may die. So a stabilized exit is when you have a less six month average that makes sense and is stabilized essentially a flat line and the pro for this is you're going to get the maximum potential price that you can get for this site. The con is that the con for the buyer is that they don't have that growth curve and then they start asking you questions on hey, how do you think I can grow this site? Do you see any opportunities for growth? Because you know that buyer is trying to figure out how can they take this and 2X 10X it, right? So be ready to answer those type of questions. So there's pros and cons to both. In my personal opinion, I like to sell sites on a growth curve the midway exit. A few reasons for that is one, the buyer is happy. I want to have a buyer because I want people to get more interested in this industry but simultaneously have repeat buyers. I want the individual to come back to me for my next deal, right? And also risk management. I am an individual who likes the growth curve and when things stabilize, I personally get bored. I know that from my perspective. So if I sell a bit earlier, I'm okay with that. I can take that funds and buy another site that I can then grow as well. So that's my personal opinions. Now how it works for you, it all depends. Once you do a few of these, you kind of understand how your personality and how you work and just go from there. In short though, in this exit optimization phase, it's to prepare the site for an exit. It depends on when you want to exit but the goal is to have a last six month average that is higher than the last six month average that you bought at. If it's not higher or it's very close, you're just going to get your money back without any return on investment. So make sure you're growing the site which is the primary driver for actually optimizing for an exit.

---

title: 16. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/stage-5-exit-execution-544/


### Transcript

In stage four, we talked about preparing your site for an exit. That's when to sell it, how to structure the site, essentially, to get that exit that you want. Now we're in stage five, which is executing the exit, actually listing the site for sale. There's some things to know about, apart from just giving it to a broker or finding a buyer yourself. We want to strategically sell a site, but depending on the price point of your valuation, it's very critical to understand how long it will take and what kind of buyers are readily available at that price range. Now, I had some sweet spots that are based on years of experience that combine the time on market, essentially the time the site sits on the market for sale and the cash payout that you can potentially obtain. So if a site's less than $10,000 in valuation, you have a large, large buyer pool. I have sold sites less than $10,000 within a day, but usually you can expect within 48 hours you'll find a buyer if you're looking in the right spots. You'll find a buyer and you can set up the transaction and close within a week. Now, if you're less than $50,000, greater than $10,000, that's also a large buyer pool. A lot of people are looking for that cash flow to supplement their day jobs. Those sites can be sold within one to two weeks, sometimes even quicker if the site is a very high quality. Now when you get up to that range of $50,000 to $200,000, you're getting a more sophisticated buyer pool. It's less buyers in that range. And it's going to take about three plus weeks to sell. Now for these types of sites that are $50,000 to $200,000, it's really important if you don't have a network of buyers, which many people don't, or if you don't know somebody who's looking for a site at that range, you will have to take the help of a broker or a marketplace to list it and then get it sold. So at that range, brokers like Empire Flippers, Motion Investors Club will take on your site, do the due diligence and vet the site, list it on their marketplace, so I should brokerage, and then buyers will come by if some buyer is interested, they'll take a fee. So definitely think about using a more managed service if you're selling a site $50,000 to $200,000. And expect about three plus weeks to sell. I've seen sites that sell very quickly, but really on average it's a month or so higher. Now $200,000 and above, right, this is very small size buyer pool. You're looking at bigger investment funds, private equity companies, or individuals with a lot of capital, but again, you are getting your amount of buyer pool to a very small number here. Now it's going to take a long time to find a buyer here, four plus weeks, it could take months, and the individual who's going to buy it is going to do much more due diligence. So definitely be ready for that and be aware that it's going to take time. And again, you should be using a broker in this situation because they have these bigger buyers, which you can't just find on a Facebook group or asking somebody, they're kind of using brokers exclusively. So one thing I want to take away is you don't always need to sell to essentially execute and exit, right? You can sell or reduce your time commitment by hiring a website operator or a dedicated team of virtual assistants that kind of handle the day-to-day tasks. In the first stages of website flipping stage one through four, it was you being the operator of that business doing the day-to-day tasks or outsourcing the day-to-day tasks. However, if your site is big enough, it's generating enough cash flow, you can hire an operator, somebody who's trained or understands niche websites, or somebody you can train up to do the day-to-day management with you being the growth strategist. So that's a very good way to sort of exit that business if you think it still has potential when it's helping you generate a lot of cash flow for other projects and essentially still keep it in your portfolio. So in short, know what valuation you fall under and then understand how long it will take and understand what the brokers are asking for and how to structure your site so that you get the maximum exit. The one thing I'll leave you with, lastly, is a lot of people try to get the highest valuation, the highest multiple. However, it's not always that simple. I'd rather reduce the multiple such that I get a faster sale instead of sitting on the market for many months. The difference between a 35x multiple and a 33 is, could be thousands of dollars, however thousands of dollars quicker in my pocket, right? That a buyer is willing to happily would buy a 33x and we can close the deal. I can move on and they can get started to grow the site. I'll leave you with that.

title: 17. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/2-case-studies-of-website-flips-801/


### Transcript (Part 1)

Now let's look at a case study. I acquired a site in April 2019 for $32,000 on Flippa. It was earning about $800 to $1,000 a month so I paid approximately let's say $32 to $35X multiple. Pretty average. However I saw a lot of openings to grow the site. A lot of easy wins sexually. So after buying the site, this is this life cycle that the site followed. So from May, June, each time period we stabilized the site. We added a lot of easy wins. We optimized the content. We went through that process and we took the site from earning $800 to $1,000 to about $5,000 average. So it was a very big transition from $1,000 to about $5,000. During that phase we're not really exponentially growing the site. We are not adding a ton of content. We're really just optimizing what's available on the site during that time period. Then we entered stage 3 which was the growth phase. And during the growth phase, it was about six month time period. We are adding new content. We are doing conversion rate optimization on the pages that are getting traffic. We are finding new revenue sources and essentially increasing the site's last six month average during that time period. Then we have increased the site from earning $1,000 to up to $10,000. That's what we're thinking about the exit in stage 4. That exit the thought process started in about January, December time period when we're thinking what should we do with this and decided that hey the site has grown. We have other interests and so let's just sell it. We took a six month average which came out to about $6,000 average. We sold the site to an investor in January 2020 for $194,000. Again, this is a great example of a flip. It was done in less than I would say, I think about 10 months, 11 months. We took a site from earning $1,000 up to a $6,000 average. Buyer was getting a site that was actually earning much more than that in the Consecling months. The average was lower but the actual revenue month to month, the future month was actually the $9,000 to $11,000 range. They were happy. We were happy because we got a huge return on investment on this deal. This is an example of a case study where the website flip lifecycle is put into practice from stage one or the latest stage five.


### Transcript (Part 2)

This case study is an outdoor site that is earning now about $3,500 per month average. We purchased this site from Flipup. It was earning $300 but generating about 75,000 plus page views per month. We purchased it for $23,000 which is a 75x multiple. Now that's significantly higher than the market. Market is about a 35 to 40, 45 max. We paid a significant sum higher for this site. The reason for that is to close the deal because we saw a lot of easy wins. It was also listed on Flipup as an auction and we didn't want a bidding war to begin and also we're confident in our abilities to really 10x this site and therefore we're willing to pay a premium just to close the deal early on so that we can get started. So this was a great example of a site that had no conversion rate optimization done. Didn't have display ads or nor any SEO done to the site. It was created by hobbyists who love the topics on the site. We're experts at the topic but not necessarily how to monetize it or how to optimize it for the search engines. So again we applied stages one through five on this site and you can see once we bought the site after about them within a month we took the site to about $2,300 from $300 and that was just adding affiliate links comparison tables doing CRO on the pages, adding display ads, the very simple things and then the growth phase started. This was a 10-year-old domain with DRF domain rating of 50 very aged very high quality backlinks so we were able to feel that fire right. So from months to the 6th April to let's say October we added a lot of content to the site. We added about a hundred articles a month month to the site to really get it going. We tried new email techniques, we tried email marketing because it was an e-shop email marketing works very well and that's why you see the boost in July August September and that's when we did email marketing we generated about two to three thousand dollars extra from email marketing. However over time we realized that email marketing is not our forte it was not easy to keep up with it and also this was during the pandemic time period in 2020 and the topics on the site kind of resonated and email marketing was converting very well but over time we were seeing conversion goes down and the ROI from that go down so we just go went back to what our expertise is which is such an optimization. So that's why you see the drop in the revenues and it's coming back down to the actual average based on just passive organic traffic which is about three and a half thousand dollars. Sometimes peaks at you know four thousand but three to half is the average. So the growth phase is done on this site we've had a lot of content what we're right now doing is in exit optimization. So as of today October 2021 we still own the site it's in our portfolio. We are holding on to it optimizing it for Q4 2021 where you know we'll see the boost in revenues and potentially think about selling this in 2022 sometime. We're not 100% sure if we want to sell it that's something we already have have an operator on board which is what I talked about that it's not necessary that you actually fully sell the site you can find an operator and put them on the site so they manage a day-to-day. So that's our exit optimization essentially that we have found an operator so they manage it for very small amount of percentage of the total revenues and so that is technically our exit and but the full exit may happen in 2020 sometime if we feel like we have another project we want to put more funds into more on new acquisition that we want to do. So it's a great case study here of taking a site that's earning three hundred dollars up to three and a half thousand dollars. We actually got our money back on the original twenty three thousand dollars in seven months by reinvesting as well so it was a very quick return on investment and so the showcases the power of website buying and selling if done right.

title: 18. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/using-brokers-444/


### Transcript

One of the most common ways to finding websites to buy is to use brokers. Now there are a lot of players in the content website industry. Those include Empire Flippers, Quiet Light International, Quiet Light Brokage, FI International, Investors Club, Motion Invest, the website Flip Brokage, and several others. Now there's pros and cons. I use brokers to buy sites. However, I tend to go towards buying sites directly from sellers because it gives me the most room for negotiation. However, if you're struck down time, you need assistance. Brokage is the best place to get that, because they're going to kind of hand hold you through the process. Now the pros of using a broker, you get a variety of deal full, you know, a variety of niches, a variety of monetization structures, and different types of businesses you can buy. They have a whole range of that, which is great to find the right one for yourself. Now they vet the deals. Many of the brokerages go through the data, go through the domain history, go through all of that, and then review it, and if it meets the minimum criteria, list it on their brokerage marketplace for sale. Now that doesn't mean they did proper do diligence. Do diligence is still on you because it has to meet your criteria for buying. Don't ever let a broker tell you we did all the do diligence. It's good to go. You can buy it. You have to do your own do diligence. Just remember one thing that brokers get paid when a site sale. So you can just imagine that do diligence is at your own risk, and they're going to walk away with the 10 to 20 percent commission rates. They collect all of the data, screenshots, profit and loss spreadsheets, question and with the sellers. You can analyze all of that at once, saving you a ton of time. They actually help with the website migration. They transfer the site to your host. They change out of affiliate links. They give you some time to inspect if the site is working properly or not. And they also have escrow services. So they hold the money from your end until you're happy and you can really take the and release that to the seller. So it gives you a buffer. Many brokers have their own in-house escrow services, but most use escrow.com to do the, to protect both the buyer of the seller. But there are a lot of cons. While even though the pros listed higher, there's the cons are pretty significant. Now, high commission rates, right? 5 percent up to 20 percent commission rate on a on a content site sale. So if you're buying a site for, you know, $100,000, a broker could get up to $20,000 of that. From here, essentially, the seller gets $80,000. So with that said, that's why market multiples have been increasing. Brokers are increasing the market multiples trying to get the buyers to buy. Whereas a savvy buyer is going to negotiate the multiple down to a more reasonable level as a final sale price. Now, the biggest issue I, in my opinion, the other two can be sort of mitigated that you can negotiate the multiple and, you know, challenge them on that. The challenge I have is that you don't actually have a direct contact with the seller. You're not talking to the seller. You're talking at somebody at the brokerage, maybe a manager or an analyst who's relaying information back and forth. Therefore, you may ask a question and, you know, they may change the tone of that question or whatever and then get an answer. And so you're not getting the full representation in most cases. Now, that also causes less room for negotiations. You may make an offer which the broker may reject. I'm saying that's too low, even though maybe the seller might have accepted it. So these kind of things cause a lot of back and forth and is a pretty big con for a broker. Regardless though, brokers get the lion's share of the deals that hit the marketplace. Sellers want a more, beginner sellers want a more handhold process where they don't have to find a buyer because it's not that easy. So brokers get a lot of the deal for. So it's in your best interest to keep track of each marketplace. Subscribe to their email list, go through their deals, do practice, do diligence on deals that you may not buy, but you can just practice on them to see how well you come out. And just keep an eye on them because obviously again, they have a lion's share of the deals.

title: 19. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/using-marketplaces-348/


### Transcript

The previous lesson I talked about using brokers. Now there's another subset of a broker called just marketplaces. Marketplaces is where it's a good medium between doing off-market deals and working with brokers because with marketplaces, you work directly with the seller. You can contact them, you can negotiate, you can understand their reasoning for selling, you can get direct data from them instead of a middleman. But there's still pros and cons, let's go over them. Pros, as I said, you can get the deal in the best deal possible in terms of a sale price. You can negotiate directly, you can offer whatever price you want and engage what the seller really needs and then make a deal. Again, marketplaces will have much more deal flow than even brokers because brokers may be less than 100 deals and they only list 10 of them because each deal has to be well vetted, minimum revenue requirements, minimum traffic requirements, minimum age, or is it a marketplace like Flip-A? It's just like eBay. Anybody can list anything for sale. It's up to you as a buyer to vet so you can get access to a lot of different types of websites. As I said, initially you can directly negotiate with the seller, which is the biggest plus point in my opinion. And also because of marketplaces and there's no middleman and there's no handholding, the fees are much lower. For example, Flip-A is 10% from the seller's proceeds. And micro acquires another marketplace that actually doesn't have any broker fees. So that means that the seller doesn't have to mark up the sale price in order to cover the fees they can sell it more reasonable multiples, which is not the case with brokers. Now, there's a lot of cons with marketplace but there's no due diligence. There's no middleman broker providing a data dump of all the screenshots and P&L. There's nobody going over the backlink profile. That's all on you. A seller can make any claims since there's no revenue verification, website verification. The seller can state whatever they want in the description and in the data and it's on you to verify. You have to do the website migration or the seller has to help you with the website migration. There's no middleman here. Again, because sellers are just listing at their own discretion, most sellers don't even know what kind of data to provide. So there's not enough screenshots. There's not a P&L spreadsheet. So it's up to you to push the seller in order to get all of that data. Most sellers are willing to go and get all that for you because they're actually listing on the marketplace thus they want to sell. So they're willing to go through that process. But there is a subset of sellers that just won't provide any more data. And those are the deals you just walk away from. I found my best deals through Flip-Up. Flip-Up has what's called market share, essentially whenever somebody Googles, how do I sell my business? Flip-Up comes up and therefore most people start there. And this allows for deals that are created by hobby bloggers, content which as they are created by real experts and now they want to sell. And those are the best deals you're going to get because high quality content, aged website, no shady SEO tactics done. So in my opinion, marketplaces are the best. If you have time to sift through all the listings, if you have limited time, brokers work best for you.

title: 21. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/off-market-deal-tactics-253/


### Transcript

So in the previous slide I talked about some non-conventional ways to buy contact websites. Now I love doing off-market deals because I'm not competing with a hundred other buyers. I'm also not competing with a broker in the middle that's mediating between the two parties and also price gating essentially. So an off-market deal, how I look for deals is I analyze the search results. So if I know a target keyword that I want to dominate, let's say for the website flip.com, my target keyword is website flipping. So I can analyze the search results for the first, let's say, ten pages of Google and essentially make a short list of blogs and sites that look neglected, that looked aged, that looked like the owners don't really care for it anymore. And I actually outreach to every single one of them saying, who I am, I don't hide that I run a competitive competing site and I essentially ask them if they want to sell and that I will take care of their site and merge it into mine so that's part of a bigger brand. And I essentially make a strong offer after a bit back and forth. Now I make strong offers because again, when you do an unsolicited email to a website owner that's not even thinking about selling, they think that their websites worth a lot of money and they're going to say some random number that's very high. It's your job as a buyer to bring them back to reality and tell them what the value is of their blog and just making offerings and say, hey, I'm available to buy whenever you're ready to sell. I have received a lot of deals like this where I'm essentially the seller wants some ridiculous price. I come in at a more reasonable price and say, let me know when you're ready to sell. And within a month or two months, they think about that and they're eventually ready to sell because the site is neglected. They're not going to work on it. It's just managing the ego through this process. The success rates are very low with this. I may send 100 emails to 100 blogs. I get back only five responses and all of that. If I'm lucky, I close one. But again, success rates are very low and it's niche dependent as well. Just website owners will just ignore you on solicited email. That's just the fact. However, if you can close a deal, it's going to be a very good one. So in short, just keep this strategy in mind that if you're in the market to buy a specific niche site, then it's maybe worth your time to just outreach to all of the ones you can find that look like they've been neglected and are ranking still and maybe page to the 10 and make them an offer. Because it's going to be much cheaper than paying 50x multiple at a brokerage.
