title: 24. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/valuation-methodology-335/


### Transcript

One of the biggest questions I get when people want to buy a site is how much are paid for this website or how much do I sell this website for. Now when evaluating a website there's two levers that you can manage. One is the average profit and the other is the industry monthly multiplier. Now, content websites are sold based on a monthly multiplier whereas SaaS and e-commerce and other types of businesses would be an annual multiplier. However, the change between the two is very simple calculation. Now the average profit is usually done over a timeframe depending on the website. Mostly it's the last six months average. If it's a seasonal website it can be over a 12 month period. Now how do you determine the profit? Essentially the revenues of the business over that time period, average revenues on a monthly basis, minus the average operational costs. Very simple but in a future slide I'm going to cover exactly what that means. Now let's take an example. Let's say that the monthly multiplier range today is about 35 to 45 times your monthly average profit. Now it's a big range I'm just giving an example here. If your site makes $1,000 per month an average profit then you can sell the site for 35,000 to 45,000 dollars. That's how simple the calculations are. There are nuances between each of these different levers which we will cover. So the simple calculation, the basic formula is your last six months profit multiplied by the monthly multiple equals your website valuation. Now as I said in the past how you determine the last six month profit is it over a six month period, a three month period, a nine month or a 12 month. That is up for debate and sometimes people use a 12 month, sometimes people use a six month. I like to follow a six month strict rule here on sites that are non seasonal and then 12 months on sites that are seasonal. You will come across sites that are young. Let's say they're less than two years old, maybe one year old. They don't have that history. So taking a 12 month average wouldn't make sense because maybe the first six months it didn't earn anything. Or it's growing across the last three months and so taking a six month average may not make sense. So it's up to the buyer and seller to figure out what kind of time period to use for the data and come to a conclusion. Now the monthly multiple though is based on whatever the industry is following. Right now as of November 2021 it's 35X is the base monthly multiplier and that can go up to 40, 50X depending on the site. It can also go down to 20 to 30X depending on how low performing the site is. So it really depends. But if the site has been stable, traffic is growing. There's no negative SEO or bad backlinks. Then a 35X multiple is very fair. So that's how you do a simple evaluation calculation. That is how the contact industry does the evaluation. Now there's as I said differences between how the calculations are done, which will cover in subsequent slides.

That's what I call an operation P&O or operating P&O. The difference here is you have capital costs and operating costs per niche site. Just like any business has, let's take an example. Real estate, you have capital costs which you ingest into the property that increases value. You may add a new kitchen. You may add new bathrooms. You may redo the roof. That all adds value to your property. Then you have operating costs which are once the property has been stabilized, you have costs to maintain. It could be insurance that could be electricity, water, all of those things. You just operate and make sure the building or properties working to its full potential. Same with websites. Websites have costs that are incurred for growing the site. We call those CAPEX or capital expenditures. Essentially, that's a bulk amount of content that's added to the site. Let's say you invest a couple thousand dollars into content, some money into backlinks, some money into testing a new traffic source of some kind. That's all CAPEX. That is not for the day-to-day maintenance. You didn't need to do that in order for the site to just operate at what it's doing today. You're doing that in the hope that it's going to grow, either when you sell it or sell the site. But then there's OPEX, which is operating expenditures. That's the cost we talked about in the previous slide, operating and maintaining that site at the current traffic and revenue levels. As we cap here, you don't need CAPEX for day-to-day operations. And so, you do not need to include CAPEX into your P&L when you go to sell a site. If you are selling a site and the buyer asks for how much did you invest in content, that's a separate discussion. That does not factor into valuations. There is no reason why you should have a lower valuation because you invested early on when you maybe growing the site $5,000, $10,000. That should not factor in. That's your investment into the site. The buyer is going to pay based on a multiple of revenues minus the operating costs. Same thing when you go to buy, it is wrong to assume to have the seller add all of their growth costs into the P&L, such as you get a better value which when you go buy, it works both ways. So in short, let's summarize. Your revenue should be based on passive income sources. Do not include sources that are one time. For example, a lot of people sell media packages and guest posts and stuff. Those should not be included. Those are one time. You can let the seller know, the buyer know that that's an option, that there is a revenue stream, but that doesn't get factors into the valuation. Then you have costs. Make sure they're operating costs only, not growth costs. With those, you can take the revenues, passive revenues, minus the operating costs and you get what's called your earnings, your profit. For example, we bought a site on Flip-A for 75x multiple. So it was about $23,000 and a 75x multiple. Now that is ridiculous by any standard in terms of the multiple because it was only earning about $300 a month. However, we saw opportunity that we can buy it and within one month increase it to $3,000, which is exactly what we did. So we were willing to close with whatever price the seller said to just take it off the market at a 75x. They were not willing to negotiate it. But we saw that we can do XYZ growth strategies and essentially 10x the revenues in one month. And that's what we did. And therefore, we didn't really care what the multiple was. Then when you're going to an negotiation, you've kind of understood what your max price is. Just know what you're willing to pay based on how much time it's going to take for you to get a good ROI of your time and money. Now on negotiating and typing that I like to use to justify my offer, let's say that I believe the site's valuation to be $20,000. It's an example site. And the seller is once $25,000. By you providing that to the buyer, they get confidence that, okay, here I can just run my numbers very quickly. You'll be amazed how many sellers don't actually put together a P&L spreadsheet. Document the highlights and the setbacks. There is nothing. Everybody knows every business is not perfect. Every content site is not perfect. So, there's nothing to hide. A savvy buyer is going to figure out what's wrong with the site by doing due diligence. So, just document the good things which everybody does, but document some of the issues with the site. Be honest and upfront, you're going to get a sale. Document why you were selling. This is a common thing that all sellers ask. So, might as well as a buyer's ask. Or it's that you don't see opportunity or you've maximized your potential on this site, your expertise, then just say that, right? And then create a list of easy-win growth opportunities. If you can document a list of opportunities that you see that you may not have time to implement, that's going to give confidence to the buyer that there are opportunities here for me to continue growing this site. After that, the transaction is 100% complete and the buyer has the assets, the seller has the funds, a successful deal. I love to use escrow.com. escrow.com is one of the most premier and well-known escrow services for online assets. A lot of people use it for buying digital assets. Now, it's very easy to use escrow.com if the buyer seller are in most of the countries that have safe banking standards. title: 53. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/creative-finance-structures-earnout-examples/


### Transcript

So I want to touch on creative finance structures for buying online businesses. So this is a very critical topic right now because in my opinion, as somebody who's been doing this for 15 years of buying online businesses, growing them and selling them, it's now a buyer's market. It used to be a seller's market, which means that a seller could ask for a valuation and get a full cash offer. And now that has shifted more to a buyer's market where a buyer can be more creative with how they pay out for the deal. So there's three ways that, and there's a lot of ways, right? But the three ways I have seen in the online business acquisition world to structure a deal. So one is earnouts and the other is seller financing. So earnouts, it's a structure where the business owner, the seller, obtains future compensation if the business achieves a certain goal. Now that's tied to some financial goal. For example, let's say the business used to do $5,000 a month. And if an earnout structure could be, if it hits $3,500 a month, then the seller will get X dollars, right? Whatever that is. And then essentially, they keep hitting these targets and the seller keeps getting their payouts. Another one that I love to do is earn out tied to a performance of the business. Not both are tied to performance, but one I'm about to talk about is tied to a percentage of revenue. So it's similar to the above, but you pay the seller a percentage of revenue each month. So it could be 75% of revenue each month. So if a business unfortunately dips after you acquire it, you pay out a certain percentage each month and you try to recover it. Both parties are very aligned in that sense. The next one is seller financing. It's less common. It's very common in real estate. So seller finances where you get a promise every note, a mortgage note, where the seller gets paid back, principle, and interest on the business over a certain term. So terms could be five to seven years, which is usually the case. And they get like 10% interest payments on top of that. So the challenge there is there's not really an alignment of incentives. It's sometimes very favorable to sellers for that because they're essentially getting guaranteed payments, but that doesn't mean that business is doing well because the seller financing note, the essentially the promiseary note, is separate to the business. So you have to pay that no matter what. So I don't like to do that. I like to do earn outs and I'm going to talk about two example deals that my team and I have done recently. So this one is a software as a service business. We've been negotiating on this through 2023 and just close end of 2023. And so the structure is a minimum down payment plus earn outs plus guaranteed payments. What does that mean? So let me break it down. The valuation was $40,000. Now, when you're doing earn outs and structures like this, they protect you as the buyer. So it has to be good for the seller as well. So the seller in open market on a cash deal valuation wise probably couldn't have gotten 30 to 35,000 for this. So we gave them a premium, right? And now the seller also has to understand that the buyer me, I'm going to ask for something in return for the premium. So something to protect myself. So what we did, we put $1,000 down payment. That's very low. Usually on an earn out deal, it's 60 to 75% down payment. We put very low because seller was very happy that we're going to take this over and grow it. But that's a unique situation for me. That's not the case for everyone. For the first two months, I'm paying 75% of revenues for the first two months. Now it's a software business, something we have not acquired in the past. It requires developers. It requires some experience in the backend systems coding, right? So we were a little hesitant on that because we just don't have that experience. And so I said, okay, the first two months, let's do 75% of revenues. So if the business is doing well, you're going to be involved. The seller will be involved to help me during those first two months transition, put a developer in place that we hire and just be around, right? Because they're going to get paid 75% of the revenues. Then the remaining months, essentially, we pay fixed payments, $6,500 a month until the $40,000 valuation is hit. So let's say I buy it the first two months, it's not doing too well because let's say, we're going to get the software broke and we're just not able to fix it. Cellar ghosted us. Or one of the biggest things with smaller SaaS companies is key man risk. Key man risk, meaning the seller is very involved in the marketing, the development. And they walk away and the business just stops working because the identity of the software was tied to the seller. And so we didn't want that. That's why we did the two months seller financing, a seller structure like that. And so let's say the first two months we buy and it just doesn't generate enough revenue, right? It just dips. So that means that the first two months we pay out very little, sellers incentivize to kind of help us. Because even though we're going to pay the six, we're always going to pay the $40,000, right? That's the structure we did. It's just we structured it in a way where it's breaking, broken out over multiple months. And so the first two months we pay out nothing because that's it, the business doesn't do well. And so we're going to get $6,500 over a longer period of time, right? Whereas if the business was doing well the first two months, maybe we double it, right? For whatever reason, they're going to get their money back, the $40,000 much quicker. So it's just about a timeline thing. If they get it back in six months versus let's say 14 months, sort of 16 months, whatever the time period is, right? The next one was an agency. We're still in the negotiations for this. But the structure is a little bit simpler. It's a down payment and it earn out tight to performance. So $10,000 down, valuation was $100,000. And we're going to pay 75% of monthly revenues until that valuation amount is achieved. Now this one is a little different, no guaranteed payments because this agency is on a down trend. The seller just doesn't want to continue running it. So they know it's going to, if they don't continue, if you don't continue running an agency which is a service-based business, it's just going to die. They won't be happy, they'll quit and that's it. So they knew that this is not going to work for them long term. So they reached out to us, say, would you like to take this over? And they were hoping to a very creative structure that's very favorable to us as the buyer. Where 75% of the revenues paid out monthly. So essentially we come in as an operator until we take it over. So we're going to operate the business, generate the revenue, pay them back 75% until the 100,000 valuation is achieved and then they walk away. Now for whatever reason, if the down trend continues with our operations, market is not doing well, they're going to get 75% of the revenues until they are essentially paid back. But that could take multiple years now rather than like less say 12 months. It could take three years if the business just keeps down training it. We'll continue operating it, but at certain point we might go back and renegotiate and say, look, this business is dead. There's nothing here. We'll just let us sit or maybe just take it over again, right? Worse case. So this is a very aligned structure where seller and buyer both understand that this business is going to die. It's in the best interest for me to take this over and try to revive it. Essentially, I've turned around deal and hopefully, hopefully the seller gets paid a fair price over a 12, 24 month period. So it's in the interest of both parties. So in short, these are some creative financial structures. I love doing these structures because it protects me, protects my downside risk, right? Now here are some caveats. Not every seller is going to be interested in this. We've gone through a four to five year period where everybody bought deals with cash, especially if you look at Empire and Flippers, quiet, light, F the international flip out every deal, majority of deals under $500,000 was mostly cash. So people that were used to that, they have in sellers, I said, they're used to that. They haven't gotten to the point where they think, okay, maybe there isn't enough cash buyers out there from my deal. I need to work with a buyer to be more creative so that they're willing to still take over the business and I can walk away. So this is a very favorable structure for a buyer. So you have to make the structure very favorable for the seller. You either have to pay more. So it's the open market value evaluation for a business of $100,000. You might need to pay $120,000, right? But you have to structure where you're protected. You're willing, I'm willing to pay a higher valuation. The valuation is just a number, right? I'm willing to pay a higher valuation if the business keeps performing, right? The risk is that business does not keep performing and then I'm stuck with $100,000 loss. So if the business keeps performing, I have no problem paying a few extra $20,000 a seller over a time period, right? No big deal. So essentially you have to convey to the seller that you are the right buyer for the business and that they're going to get paid out whenever they do, right? And so that means you have to have expertise in the business industry. You have to showcase your expertise, your team and have confidence when you're going into the deal. So this is not for everybody. If this is your first time buying an online business and you've never done this before, sellers will be very skeptical whether you are the right person and they're willing to take a risk on you. So be aware that this is not for everybody, but if you can, whenever you're going into negotiation, ask if they're willing to do an earn out structure, ask if they are willing to do this. You never know, right? You never know. So utilizing creative financial structures, put them together. When you make an offer, you can do a cash offer, which is lower valuation. Let's say the open market valuation is $100,000. You can say, hey, I'm willing to pay you $75,000 cash or I can pay you $110,000, but it's going to be a seller, earn out structure like this. So be creative. You can do multiple offers, see what the seller is willing to work with you on. Some are willing to wait longer to get paid on, some just want the cash. So hopefully that was helpful. Let me know if any questions.

In this video, I'm going to talk about what side operators. Now if you are consistently buying selling sites and building up a portfolio, it's going to come to a point where you may have a site that has more growth opportunities and you want to keep it in your portfolio because it's fairly stable in a good niche, but you cannot put more time into it because you want to take the cash flow from that and acquire a new site and essentially build a real business here of acquiring businesses, growing them and then selling them over time. So you need what's called a website operator. So in this lesson, I'm going to go over a few aspects of a website operator. For example, why should you hire one, the high level roles a website operator does, how do you incentivize these individuals, case studies and a job description that I use to kind of put together the roles for the operator and hire? Let's get started. Why should you even hire an operator? Now, my philosophy is a website is either being worked on or it's being ready to be sold. There's nothing in between where you're just letting it sit. I mean, you're always doing something to it, either updating content, adding new content, adding internal something has to happen and it's either that or you're going to just sell it. Now, I have many reasons why I bring on an operator here are the ones that resonate with me. Now, life happens, there's no time. You buy a site, maybe your family, obligations there, your day job, whatever, just don't have time. So it's a good idea to bring on an operator that knows what they're doing. Now the other thing is you want to buy new sites. This is my situation where I always have shiny objects in there. I always need to go and buy more sites, try new things, test new things. And so I need what sort of operators are going to manage my portfolio while I go up and buy more. Other things is that everybody has their own expertise. My expertise is conversion rate optimization and semi expertise and searching it optimization. I'm not an expert in SEO. I have the tools that I have. Now, when you could on a site that you want to keep on your portfolio, you may have exhausted all your expertise and just cannot make it grow. So you need a fresh set of eyes. You need a fresh set of expertise to come in and grow that site. The next one is, you don't really want to sell a site. You see that there's a lot of opportunities for growth, maybe a lot of low competition keywords, maybe some pages have not been optimized for earnings. Maybe those are new revenue stream that needs to be tested and all that takes time. So you see these opportunities, you have a plan for that, you just don't have the time to execute. And so that's when you can bring on an operator that can execute on your strategies and grow. And the last one is rare, but it has happened to me in our giving example. You've tried to sell the site and there's just no buyers available. For example, I have a site in a dating and relationship niche. It's about $10,000 a month, about $400,000 valuation. We have gone through all the brokers, we have gone through Flipup. We have not had one offer on it. It's just a niche that not a lot of people want to enter and at that price level. So what do we do in that situation? We have to keep it, right? And either we spend our time growing it or we put an operator on which we did hire an operator and I'll cover that in the case studies in the next few slides. So that's the main reasons why hiring operators always comes down to a balance of time, commitment, interest, commitment on your end. And then what's the opportunity for that site? So what are the roles, right? The website operator are high level. They manage the day-to-day of your website. These tasks they can include keyword research, outlines, assigning to writers, getting the content back from the writers, editing that, formatting and posting on the website, publishing it essentially. Then keeping track of which articles are getting traffic so you can start, they can start adding affiliate links, call to action buttons, call to action boxes, pop-ups, which is the conversion rate optimization aspect of your website. And then it could be, let's say that you find a new revenue stream, you want the operator to try it out. They're going to be the ones testing and collecting the data and seeing what's working and what's not. They're also doing on-page SEO. They're improving the headers, they're improving the keywords, they're improving the internal links. They're adding data to articles, etc. They're also obviously the simplest of these tasks is to keep the website up to date, make sure it's malware, virus-free, make sure the plugins are up to date, make sure the theme is still working, etc. So high-level, that's what they're really doing and this kind of summarizes what niche content website needs on a daily basis. Now where to find an operator. This is actually the most common question I ever get asked on this topic is where, which figure can I find these, you know, eight class operators? I'll caveat this slide and say it's not easy. If there is no place, you just go and hire an operator. Because an operator's expertise encompasses various topics, right? They need to understand SEO. They need to understand niche websites. They need to understand conversion rate optimization. They need to understand the different revenue streams available. There's just so many different tactics here that need to come together in one individual. Now one thing I have seen is, and you'll see this too, people who operate their own sites, you won't be able to hire them because they can do it for themselves, right? It's hard to find a good operator because they're doing it themselves. So we can figure out other ways to kind of work through that, but let's first cover where to find an operator. If you have VA's on your team, virtual assistants that have been doing a lot of these semi-tasks for you, give them an opportunity to take over the entire site, see what they do, give them a month's chance, one month, two months, and then just hand over the site to them, tell them to do pretty much everything and ask you for help. It's much easier to train up somebody who has been working on a site at a smaller level and train them up because now you own that asset. You can hire them full time to manage three to four sites for you. Another thing is, you could hire an SEO expert. There's a lot of people who have SEO background. They work at big companies, they work at their in-house SEO team, they work at agencies, and they want to probably branch out and build their own portfolio and learn how to build content sites from scratch. Most SEO experts in-house were hired. They didn't build the business from scratch. They were hired to continue building the business. These are two different expertise. However, they know how to grow and therefore you can hire them and teach them, okay, this is the in-house of a content website. Another is posting in Facebook groups. This is how I find some of my operators is just making a post in a Facebook group that I'm looking for somebody who has expertise in content websites, has built a few, maybe even sold a few and I'm looking to pay them to manage a few of my sites. Very simple. You get a lot of leads. Some of them are really bad, but you know, the needle and the haystack does show up. And again, you can hire a competent individual, maybe they have expertise in one topic or another and you can teach them. Obviously, that's the most time-consuming part of everybody wants an operator out of the box, but obviously those are going to be more expensive. You may have to give up equity for those good operators, but if you train somebody from scratch, you can control the whole aspect and they can manage many sites for a fixed salary for example. Now one thing I would like to caveat is, don't go looking in freelance websites, upwork in these places are not ideal. There's no such role as a website operator. This is an ad hoc role. It's a combination of many roles. Very few people have that expertise and upwork is not the place you're going to find that. So how do you incentivize? Now this is very critical. In the previous slide, I said that operators that operate their own site won't operate someone else's. It's hard to find a veteran operator who's going to take over your site. So what can you do there? For a veteran operator that you really want to work with, you have to give them equity, significant equity, more than 10%, sometimes up to 30%, just to work on your site. Plus you may need to give them a stipend, monthly fixed fee payment. Now I'll get to this chart here. But so a veteran operator will need equity. Now if you have somebody up and coming, maybe they built a few sites, they're trying to learn and you are experienced, they would really value mentorship. For those people, I am willing to help them grow so they can ask me any question. They have access to me via chat. They can send me some ideas that they want to run through me. So they're essentially like a free consulting. Obviously they're not abusing it, whether it's disturbing me daily, but it could be like one or two questions a week. It's very reasonable for me to answer. But in those situations, I hire competent individuals that have built sites. They're just not to the level where I'm at or somebody who's been buying and selling a lot of sites. Now with that individual, you don't need to give them equity. You can give them a fixed salary a month. And incentive is on top of that. Now let's get to this chart here. So there's three ways to compensate an individual. You have a stipend. That's a fixed monthly salary. And then you have bonuses, incentive bonuses. I love giving bonuses and I'll explain why. And then there's mentorship. If you can combine all three of these aspects into one, you can find an individual that's willing to work for you, learn from you, and grow your site at a very reasonable compensation package. Now what about the bonuses? So what I like to do is take an average of earnings, let's say less six month average, or last 12 month average is $5,000 a month. And I say, hey, look, if you can take this to $7,000 in any given month in the future, if it hits that amount, I'll give you an $X,000 bonus. And that really motivates them to work hard. And that $X,000 is a couple of times more than their monthly salary. And I'll give you an example. For my dating niche site, the case study is coming up, but I'll just give the numbers here. For the dating niche site, I paid an individual $500 a month. They handle everything. But on top of that, I pay them if they hit a certain milestone, let's say my site's earning $10,000, the first milestone they hit was $14,000. So it's a $10,000 average and then they hit $14,000 in one month. And they did hit that. They received a $1,000 up front bonus. It's just paid out. And their monthly salary stays the same as $500. Once that $14,000 is hit, we move the milestone up to $16,000. And this is a back-and-forth conversation on what's realistic. You can't just say $30,000 and expect that to happen. You have to be realistic. And then at $16,000, I increased the bonus to $2,000 bonus. For me, that's a no-brainer. Because I don't think about it as the cash flow, I'm losing cash flow in one of those months. I'm looking at how much value did they increase the website? They increased the equity value of the website's significant because a $5,000 increase at $40,000 multiple is significant. So I'm willing to give them $2,000 to put in that effort. You can incentivize them even more. So it's a combination of that. A stipend, a bonus, and then mentorship as well. They have access to me that's questioned about their sites or what they need help with. So those combinations just think about creative ways to put these three together and you can find good operators. So some things to keep in mind. Always do a 30-day paid trial unless you know the individual, right? Some of the people I've been working with, I know through my network, I have references. So it's easier for me. People have just continuously been in the game, right? But if you're looking for somebody brand new, give them a 30-day paid trial, pay them. There's nothing free in this website industry. Every talent should be paid. No free talent, no free work here. Also I like to provide a monthly growth budget. So obviously as an operator, they're not going to spend their own salary to grow. You have to provide a stipend on top of the monthly salary that they're getting. A growth budget essentially. And you can preset that. I like to set that at about $500 average for them to use. They can use that to buy content. They can buy backlinks. They can spend that money on tools they need for that site specifically. And it's always a conversation. So they have to ask me if this makes sense. They can't just have a free will over that. Always be clear on the tasks they are responsible for. So that's the job description. I'd always make sure the job descriptions are well detailed and well laid out. Always create SOPs. If there's some specific things on the website that's different than most content websites, create a SOP. SOP is a standard operating procedure. So just a video recording or a checklist or something where they can refer back to. Another thing is make sure you gain you trust them. They will have access to your website. This means they may have admin level access to your WordPress website. So even if you don't trust them fully, you will trust them over time. But one way to protect against all of that is just have frequent backups. I have daily backups from multiple places of my site. Some are on site backups on the website, on WordPress itself. I have off-site backups. And so if for any reason something bad happens, I can just kick them out and then put up a new website, put up the same website up again. It will take time, but at least I have that. You have to give them a lot of access, a lot of logins. It's just the way the website operating industry works here. Now let's get into some case studies. So the case study here is on my dating relationship site. This site earned about, I want to say, 9 to $10,000 a month in around June, July, August time period. I realize that I need an operator because I just have too much other projects going on. And so we hired an operator. I paid this individual $500 a month, fixed regardless of performance. And then bonuses, if they hit milestones. For example, one of their milestones was first to take the site to $12,000 and then take the site to $14,000. And so this individual started in July, 2021. And immediately they were able to boost the site in one month, all the way up to about $10,500 as you see here on the chart. And then within a few months in October, they boosted the site up to about $14,500. And so they've got two bonuses from me. Their fixed salary of $500 remained the same. And they didn't do much. I mean, it's not like they did something drastic that I didn't do. They just brought a fresh set of ice and a fresh set of energy into the site. Specifically what they did is they did a CRO audit. So conversion rate optimization audit to improve the CTA's call to actions and the call out boxes, et cetera. So essentially they went through the whole site looking at which pages are getting traffic and improving those. For somebody like me who has many websites, it's kind of, I don't feel motivated to go in and update a lot of the content with affiliate links and whatnot. Because I just have another project that I'm more interested in. But for this individual that have fresh set of ice are more motivated. They can quickly get in there, look to Google Analytics and see what needs to be improved. They also did a number two here on page SEO improvements. So they did better interlinking. They improved on navigation. They improved the keywords on the specific pages to get them the rank better by going through Google Search Console and finding opportunities. They also obviously number three added new content. Now the thing with this website was they didn't add much content. They added 12 articles. So those 12 articles did not make the boost in revenue, right? They found opportunities, what's up, what I call easy wins within the site itself with existing content and existing traffic to take it from the 10K all the way to the 14K. Now for me, this was a no brainer easy win. I increased the value on my site now. I added a lot more equity value on my site. My revenue is up. I can afford to pay them long term. And so it's a win win for everybody. So the next site case study is on my outdoor site. So on the outdoor site, my mantra is that a website is a living and breathing entity. It needs upkeep. Whether the growth happens or not to keep things stable, you just need that upkeep. Which I talked about previously. Now I onboarded an operator, paid them the same $500 a month within Centives as well. They were onboarded I think late October 2021. Now from my perspective, I didn't see many easy wins on this site. I just needed somebody to keep things going, keep adding content, doing on page SEO, adding affiliate links, affiliate tables, etc. And so what they did was just kept things stable. And to me, that's the value. There's value in keeping things stable. So you can see when they onboarded in late October, since then this traffic till today in February, the traffic has been stable. It hasn't gone down. I'm still earning the same amount of money. And to me, that's a proper operator, keeping things going as they should be. So that allowed me to focus elsewhere so that I can go buy more sites and keep maintaining that site. So the next thing is what's the job description? So in below this video, I put together a Google doc with the job description that I used, the incentive structure, the salary. You can make a copy of that, change it up to your needs and use that to share it with individuals that you think could be a good operator. Again, I don't recommend putting this up into a pork or anything. Just see if you can find people through Facebook proof. There's a huge community on each website builder on Facebook. Somebody would be interested in this, somebody who maybe wants a fix allergy because their content sites are not working on perfectly. You know, content sites take time to rent bucks when individual may want a job during that period. So anyways, to summarize, a website operator is a great asset to have. There is a certain point where it makes sense, right? Don't bring on an operator on your site's only making $1,000. I like to make sure that websites make at least $4,000 a month because that sustains a growth budget plus an operator's salary to make sure that you still have enough cash flow that it makes sense. You got to have enough cash flow after paying all these fees so that it makes sense. So ideally more than $4,000 works out well. It also just depends on what are you going to pay that operator on a monthly salary. So good luck on finding a website operator. The very valuable assets if you find a good one, keep them to your team and make sure that they take more responsibility over time, take over more sites so that you can go and build an empire of content sites.

title: 55. The Course on Website Flipping
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/google-core-updates-1250/


### Transcript

Google Core updates can be very stressful. They happen multiple times a year and they happen unannounced. So for a website buyer or seller, they're pretty impactful if primary traffic source is organic search and obviously the biggest search engine in the world is Google. So what do you do when you're buying sites during and after a Google Core update? Let's say that you hear some chatter or see the official announcement that a Google update is happening and you are looking to buy a site. What do you specifically do? So I'm going to cover some tips and what I actually do during these time periods. So why are they important? Kind of alluded to that. Content sites are impacted by Google Core updates whether positive or negative. Now again, the Google search pages, the search results, somebody always wins and somebody always loses. If you if a site gets hit, somebody else replaces it. So there's always somebody going and winning or losing. Now when you're buying sites, the buyers like myself, you, we look for positive growth after Google update and the negative growth and essentially base our decision of that. Some sellers, unfortunately, there are on the various sellers. They like to optimize their listings, essentially their for sale listings to hide the impact. I see this very often on auction sites, marketplaces, Facebook groups where obviously the site has been hit but they're trying to showcase the very good aspects of the site and sort of hide the fact that it was hit. And that's not everyone. They're honest people in this industry and there are dishonest people. It's just the way it is. You can see that in every industry. Now it's important if you're going to build a portfolio of websites or build them from scratch, whatever the case to keep track of these Google updates. Now the Twitter accounts are at Search Liaison. That is essentially Google's official account where they announce these big core updates. Also you can monitor a lot of SEO Facebook groups. If you join any of them you will see chatter. People talking about it and essentially you can kind of keep keep an eye on that. So what are the timelines of a Google update? Now most websites start shifting in rankings before an update is even announced. So the official announcement comes out from Google. You'll see some shift in traffic. Some people might be talking about it. Some people might see rankings going up down weeks before the official announcement. Now once an announcement happens, usually that happens in the Twitter account. They roll out the algorithm. They even tell you we will be rolling out the algorithm at X time period on this date. Some usually within 24, 48 hours of announcing it. After the rollout happens, it takes two weeks. That's even what Google says. It takes two weeks for a full rollout to occur. And then after the full two weeks, now this is not an official announcement, but after the two four weeks, they usually do a adjustment. Let's say their algorithm rolls out and then they collect some data and Google then makes adjustments. Maybe they made a mistake, etc. So this whole process could be four to six weeks in length. So what do you do when you're buying a website? Let's say you're looking at a website to buy. Very importantly is to get Google Analytics access and then monitor what the last 30-day traffic trend looks like. Now this is really important. And something I preach a lot is that a lot of brokers like to show data up till the last day of the last month. So for example, right now I'm recording this video as June 30th. All of June has finished, but most brokers, the biggest ones, will show data up until May 31st. Even though June has pretty much done, they don't collect that data and update the PNL, update the traffic trends, grab the Google Analytics data until the end the month is finished. So you know, a Google update happens mid-month, right? And when you're looking at a broker listing, very well be that that site looks very nice. So here's an example on the left here. This is an example site and you can see the traffic trend last four months. The last one of those May 31st and it looks very nice, right? Growing traffic, the website looks very healthy. If you're not, if you're looking at that listing itself with that data, that website looks really good. You probably want to buy it. But then that same site on the right-hand side, if you just extend the data until June 29th, June 30th, you can see that there has been a traffic trend. So it's been impacted by a Google update, but the flip on a ball, the broker listing, don't show that data up until the end of the month, right? So it's very important for you to do your due diligence, getting Google Analytics traffic and analyzing that yourself and then making a point to the seller and the broker that, hey, look, the site's been impacted. So another thing I have seen, not just from the broker-hand, but if you're doing a private deal and let's say the seller knows the site has been hit by a Google update, they may not want to give you access to Google Analytics. Now again, this is very few people, but they exist, right? If somebody's not giving you access to Google Analytics, one, it could be a trust issue, but that can be relieved by you providing more information about yourself and your background. And even after that, it doesn't work just walk away. You need Google Analytics access to analyze these traffic trends. Now, another thing you can do is if you can get access to the URL, which is pretty common, just to ask for it, you can plug in the URL into atrefs, semrosh, my go-to-tools, atrefs, and just look at the traffic trend, organic traffic trend on there. Now, obviously it's an estimate, but if Google update happened and the site has been hit, that the traffic drop is very, very steep. As you can see in these two examples here of two sites, you can see the traffic drop is very, very steep. Now, some traffic drops might be a little bit, and in that case, it might not be a Google update. It could be just one keyword dropped out of position or seasonality. But if it's, if you look, you hit a steep drop, it's a very clear sign that it was a Google update hit. Now, I will caveat one thing. One of these examples is where a Google update did not hit, but the timing was off. For example, this left one is actually a site I owned. And what happened is coincidentally, I put my site to no index. I was doing some changes to the site on a staging server, and I usually know index the site, so then my staging server doesn't index with my live server while I'm working on it. And I merged the staging site to the live sites, because I was done working on it. But I forgot to remove the no index. And in weeks later, I see that I'm losing traffic, and I realize that I know index my site coincidentally, that happened simultaneously with the Google Core update. And so from the chart, it looks like Google hit the site, but it the Google did not hit the site. I had to fix the no index and then starting to climb back up. So it's not always the case that the Google update hit, but most likely 95% of the time it is. Some weird cases like mine will happen, but again, you as a buyer, you just need to be very skeptical. So in short, just plug it in the address, check out the organic traffic search chart, and if it looks like it's been hit like that, you can decide if you want to move forward or walk away. Now some important consideration. Now these are things I do on a daily basis. Whenever I hear about a Google update, and I think you should consider a strategy around Google updates as well. The first one is do not buy websites pre-update during the update or post a bit. Now this is a six-week period. It's a very stressful multi-week period here. Your own sites could be hit. Other people are talking about a non-stop on social if you're following. It's a very, very stressful time period. And during these time periods, I try not to buy any sites. Unless they've not been impacted, but if it's been impacted, I try to walk away. So what does that mean? Pre-update means maybe if you start hearing some chatter, you can kind of stop whatever deals you're doing and just say, hey, I want to tell the buy at the seller that, hey, I want to wait a few weeks to see the impact, because I'm hearing some chatter on some Google updates. Now, when a rollout happens, when announcement happens, and if you're still looking at buying a site, just tell a seller, let's wait. There's a Google update. I really need to see the impact of this. And then two weeks post. Now this is optional. After the initial two weeks rollout period, it's finished. Google does an adjustment another two weeks. Sometimes it's a week. If there's no official time period there. And some sites actually recover during this post update. And so it's in your best interest to maybe even monitor during that time period. And if everything looks good, then you can proceed with purchasing. Now, it's important if a site was hit and you're still interested in buying that site, let's say it still has a lot of easy win opportunities, even though it's been hit. And or you see easy ways to improve the site. You should not pay on the last six month diverge or last 12 month diverge. Whatever average time period you are using in your valuation calculation. Because it's been hit. That traffic is not coming back. That's the past. What I like to do is using a trailing 30 days revenue. So ask the seller to give the updated revenues and use that last one month essentially to value the site. I also reduced the monthly multiple because a monthly multiple takes the health of the site. So 35 to 45 X approximately is a healthy site. If the site has been on a downtrend, it doesn't deserve that multiple. So you can reduce it even lower. I like to go even lower to 30. And so essentially the buyer, sorry, the seller's not getting a great deal. But at the same time, it is what it is. Another thing is if you're starting out as an SEO, I really recommend don't try to catch a falling knife. Which is if you see the no, I mean if you see traffic is decreasing and downtrending. And you don't know how to fix that. You don't have the expertise to fix that. It's best to walk away. As I said, if somebody is losing in the searchers or somebody is gaining. So there's always deals to be found. It's in your best interest not to buy a site that's on a steep downtrend due to a Google Corrupted unless you really, really know what you're doing. So all in all, a Google Corrupted is very stressful. Just make sure to monitor your own traffic. Make sure to monitor the websites that you want to buy their traffic, their a dress trends and their earnings as well. And as a protection mechanism, just try not to buy anything during that four to six week period and just monitor the sites you were looking at and just tell the seller that, hey, I want to keep analyzing because there's a Google Corrupted. Most sellers will understand. They understand the game. And so it won't have much issues. I personally have not had much issues. And obviously if you come across sellers or causing issues, just walk away because they're going to cause more trouble. So I hope that was helpful. It is a stressful time period. Just make sure that you have a clear mind and you're really thinking strategically rather than emotionally.

Hey everyone, this is Meshweak from the website Flip.com. Today I want to talk about essentially website flipping trends and see as of June 2023. So there's four things I want to cover here today. Let's get started. So liquidity is lower. So this is really important. Back in the day, 2022, 2021, I would see sites selling very, very quickly. There's a huge demand for them. Less than 30 days was kind of the average for anything listed. Less than $100,000 valuation. Liquidities lower as of June 2023. Sites have been sitting on marketplaces for much longer. I have seen this experience this myself. Three sites I have tried to sell in the past few months and they just have not moved. So I just keep them in my portfolio. One of them was listed with the big brokerage that everybody knows and I had to remove it from the brokerage because over three months it just did not so. Now with even saying that you would expect multiples to go lower. However, multiples have been the same, which is a 35x baseline multiple. Which means any content site that's trending in the right direction has earnings. There's not having any bad backlinks. Has a 35x baseline and then it adjusts from there and I have a very detailed article on my website. But usually multiples lag real time. What's going on in the marketplace. So multiples may decrease in the future. We just don't know. But it does take a long time for the multiples and the brokers to sort of say, okay, we need to lower the multiples. Number three, there's a large inventory of sites. That answer very short form questions. Like does my card do this? Does this card do that? Does this model do this? You get the point, right? It's a very, very long tail keywords. It's question-based answers. And you know, these I've been seeing a ton of these sites for sale. There's obviously more. These are always for sale even last year, but now I'm seeing more and more. And I can essentially say that that's potentially because of two reasons. One, the economy is bad. People just want to cash out, have some nest egg essentially. But maybe more of two, which is AI content generation tools, which can answer a lot of these questions in pretty much the same quality or detail as a human. So again, you know, it really depends. But one thing that really has been steady is that quality brands are still selling from premium multiples. So number three, you know, large inventory sites, large inventory of sites that are answer-ish or form questions. These are not quality sites, right? These target very, very long tail keywords in bulk and turnout content and just get hundreds of thousands of page views and monetize. Nothing wrong with that. But it's not a brand. And so these sites have essentially, they'll keep churning, they'll keep making money, but they won't be able to go to the next level as there's a real revamp, right? But and that's why they're not getting those premium multiples and the sites are sitting on marketplaces. But quality sites, let's say, aside about a specific car, right? Where you're answering long form questions, but you're also answering detailed buying, detailed buying guys, you're taking pictures, you're such a treating in like a real business. Those brands will still sell for premium multiples regardless of what the current market or the market shows because there's demand for those. So what's my plan? I am still buying content sites, but I'm using this opportunity in the market to negotiate and get a better deal. It's been a seller's market for over many, many years. Now I'm not saying it's a buyer's market just yet, but as a buyer, you have options as in sites are sitting for longer, so you have some time to think about it, time to really dig deep and do the diligence and make an offer where you're the winner in the long. That was not the case even last year. So take this opportunity, look for quality deals and make quality offers and walk away if needed, right? If the seller is not able to sell, they might come back, right? And take your offer. So in general, I don't think, I think website flipping is still a very good business model. I am still doing it. I'm buying quality brands, building them up and then selling a quality brand. And so that's my course of action. Let me know if any questions. Thanks for listening.

title: 04. The Course on Aged Domains
content:
Source: https://learn.thewebsiteflip.com/courses/the-course-on-aged-domains/what-are-aged-domains-446/


### Transcript

So what are age domains? I've characterized them with these five high level points. So age domains are essentially aged, which means they're many years old. There's no specific time period, like it's not like it has to be five years old or ten years old. It just means that at least at least one year old or old. Also, the domain has been registered previously. It had an owner in the past that owner used that website, that domain for the business purposes, a blog, or whatever. Two high quality backlinks from the previous owners. This is what's a big differentiator between a fresh domain that you register for $10 and a age domain with backlinks.
